---
title: "Exporting dplyr Animations as GIFs with Palmer Penguins"
output:
  rmarkdown::html_vignette:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Exporting dplyr Animations as GIFs with Palmer Penguins}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo      = TRUE,
  message   = FALSE,
  warning   = FALSE,
  comment   = "#>"
)

# GIF export needs webshot2 + magick + a local Chrome/Chromium. When any of
# those are unavailable (e.g. on CRAN's build machines) we still show the code
# but skip the slow rendering so the vignette always builds.
can_gif <- requireNamespace("webshot2", quietly = TRUE) &&
  requireNamespace("magick", quietly = TRUE) &&
  requireNamespace("palmerpenguins", quietly = TRUE)

# Where the generated GIFs live, relative to the rendered vignette.
gif_dir <- "gifs"
if (can_gif && !dir.exists(gif_dir)) dir.create(gif_dir)
```

## 1. Introduction

The companion vignette *Animating dplyr Verbs with NBA GOAT Data* renders the
three core animators — `animate_select()`, `animate_filter()`, and
`animate_mutate()` — **inline** as live HTML/CSS/JS.

This vignette covers the other output path: turning each of those same three
animations into a self-contained **animated GIF** with `animate_save_gif()`. A
GIF works anywhere an image works — PDF output, Word, Google Slides, a README,
or plain markdown via `![](file.gif)` — so it is the right choice when you need
a portable artifact rather than a live widget.

We demonstrate the workflow on the **Palmer Penguins** dataset, a friendly
alternative to `iris` for teaching data wrangling.

### How GIF export works

`animate_save_gif()` renders each step of an animation to a PNG frame with a
headless browser (`webshot2`), then stitches the frames together with `magick`:

| Function           | GIF helper                       |
|--------------------|----------------------------------|
| `animate_select()` | `animate_save_gif(anim, "x.gif")` |
| `animate_filter()` | `animate_save_gif(anim, "x.gif")` |
| `animate_mutate()` | `animate_save_gif(anim, "x.gif")` |

Because it needs a browser and two extra packages, GIF export is **opt-in**.
Install the suggested packages first:

```{r install-suggests, eval=FALSE}
# install.packages(c("webshot2", "magick", "palmerpenguins"))
```

`webshot2` also needs Chrome/Chromium available on your system.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2. The Palmer Penguins Dataset

`palmerpenguins::penguins` records body measurements for 344 penguins from
three species on three islands in the Palmer Archipelago, Antarctica.

```{r load-pkg}
library(animatedplyr)
library(dplyr)
library(palmerpenguins)
```

```{r peek}
penguins_df <- as.data.frame(penguins)
dplyr::glimpse(penguins_df)
```

The dataset has exactly the mix that makes the three verbs worth animating:

-   character/factor columns (`species`, `island`, `sex`) to **select**.
-   numeric columns with a clear story (`body_mass_g`, `flipper_length_mm`) to
    **filter**.
-   ratios worth computing (`bill_length_mm / bill_depth_mm`) to **mutate**.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3. `select` → GIF

Build the animation as usual, then hand the returned object to
`animate_save_gif()`. Here we keep just the identity columns and body mass.

```{r select-gif, eval=can_gif, results="hide"}
sel_anim <- animate_select(
  penguins_df, species, island, body_mass_g,
  seed = 1
)
sel_anim
# animate_save_gif(sel_anim, file.path(gif_dir, "penguins_select.gif"))
```

```{r select-embed, echo=FALSE, eval=can_gif, out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(gif_dir, "penguins_select.gif"))
```

The GIF plays two frames: the full sampled set of columns, then the dropped
columns fading away to leave `species`, `island`, and `body_mass_g`.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 4. `filter` → GIF

Same pattern with `animate_filter()`. We keep the heavier penguins
(body mass above 4000 g).

```{r filter-gif, eval=can_gif, results="hide"}
filt_anim <- animate_filter(
  penguins_df, body_mass_g > 4000,
  seed = 7
)

animate_save_gif(filt_anim, file.path(gif_dir, "penguins_filter.gif"),fps = 0.5)
```

```{r filter-embed, echo=FALSE, eval=can_gif, out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(gif_dir, "penguins_filter.gif"))
```

In the rendered frames, kept rows turn green and dropped rows turn red, then the
red rows collapse away.

### Tuning the GIF

`animate_save_gif()` exposes a few knobs: `fps` (frames per second — lower is
slower and easier to read) and `width`/`height` (pixel size of each frame).

```{r filter-gif-tuned, eval=can_gif, results="hide"}
animate_save_gif(
  filt_anim,
  file.path(gif_dir, "penguins_filter_slow.gif"),
  fps    = 0.5,
  width  = 800,
  height = 500
)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 5. `mutate` → GIF

Finally, `animate_mutate()`. We compute a bill shape ratio
(`bill_length_mm / bill_depth_mm`) — a single new column, which is exactly what
`animate_mutate()` is designed to narrate.

```{r mutate-gif, eval=can_gif, results="hide"}
mut_anim <- animate_mutate(
  penguins_df,
  bill_ratio = bill_length_mm / bill_depth_mm,
  seed = 3
)

animate_save_gif(mut_anim, file.path(gif_dir, "penguins_mutate.gif"))
```

```{r mutate-embed, echo=FALSE, eval=can_gif, out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(gif_dir, "penguins_mutate.gif"))
```

The source columns (`bill_length_mm`, `bill_depth_mm`) are highlighted, then the
new `bill_ratio` column pops into view with its computed values.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 6. The `with_animation()` shortcut also exports

`animate_save_gif()` accepts anything the animators return, including the output
of `with_animation()`. So the one-call classroom syntax exports to GIF too.

```{r with-animation-gif, eval=can_gif, results="hide"}
wa_anim <- with_animation(
  penguins_df,
  filter(flipper_length_mm > 210),
  seed = 5
)

animate_save_gif(wa_anim, file.path(gif_dir, "penguins_with_animation.gif"))
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 7. All three in one pass

When you have a folder of teaching examples, a small loop saves the whole set.
This builds one GIF per verb in a single sweep.

```{r batch-gif, eval=can_gif, results="hide"}
animations <- list(
  select = animate_select(penguins_df, species, island, body_mass_g, seed = 1),
  filter = animate_filter(penguins_df, body_mass_g > 4000, seed = 7),
  mutate = animate_mutate(penguins_df,
                          bill_ratio = bill_length_mm / bill_depth_mm, seed = 3)
)

for (verb in names(animations)) {
  animate_save_gif(
    animations[[verb]],
    file.path(gif_dir, paste0("penguins_", verb, ".gif"))
  )
}
```

Each call prints a confirmation like
`Saved 4-frame GIF to .../penguins_select.gif` and returns the output path
invisibly, so you can collect the paths if you need them downstream.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Further Reading

-   `?animate_save_gif` — full GIF export documentation, including `fps` and
    frame-size arguments.
-   `?animate_select`, `?animate_filter`, `?animate_mutate` — per-verb docs.
-   `?with_animation` — the single-call wrapper.
-   `vignette("nba-goats-animations", package = "animatedplyr")` — the inline
    HTML animation walkthrough.
-   `palmerpenguins::penguins` — dataset documentation.
